coredata.dc:title: A ratiometric carbon dots/AgInS2 quantum dots sensing probe for the kinetic determination of histamine resorting to N-PLS chemometric model
coredata.dc:creator: Castro, Rafael.C.
Páscoa, Ricardo N.M.J.
Saraiva, M.Lúcia M.F.S.
Cunha, Sara.C.
Santos, João L.M.
Ribeiro, David S.M.
coredata.dc:description: Biogenic amines are compounds that play several crucial roles in the human body but can also cause serious health problems when present at high concentrations in foodstuffs. Therefore, it is important to develop rapid, simple, cost-effective, and reliable analytical techniques for their monitoring, acting as indicators of food quality and preventing potential risks. In this sense, carbon dots and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA)-capped AgInS2 (AIS) quantum dots were used as fluorescent probes to implement a ratiometric sensing platform, coupled to chemometrics, for the kinetic fluorometric determination of histamine in foodstuffs using second-order data. The obtained data were analysed through unfolded partial least squares (U-PLS) and N-way partial least squares (N-PLS). The model optimization revealed that the application of N-PLS yielded the best results and that a kinetic spectral acquisition for 5 min was enough to ensure reliable measurements. The developed method was evaluated using spiked samples of different complex matrices (tuna, tomato and hake fish), at different concentration levels, and the obtained results, in terms of figures of merit, confirmed its suitability, accuracy and effectiveness. In fact, a relative percentage error below 10%, with a coefficient of determination for the prediction higher than 0.9, was obtained, together with a LOD and LOQ of 1.26 and 3.80 mg L−1, respectively, which are much lower than the maximum threshold value established by European Union legislation for histamine in fishery products (200 mg kg−1). These results were obtained with samples containing uncalibrated species and without using toxic reagents, which attest the suitability of the procedure. Globally, the developed methodology proved to be accurate and much simpler and faster than the reference procedures.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Ratiometric probe
N-PLS
Second-order data
Histamine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of AIS ternary QDs and carbon dots
Mechanism of PL quenching of MPA-AIS QDs upon interaction with histamine
Optimization of CDs/MPA-ternary-QDs ratiometric sensing platform

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Rafael C. Castro thanks FCT / MCTES (Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia and Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior) and ESF (European Social Fund) through NORTE 2020 (Programa Operacional Região Norte) for his PhD grant ref. 2020.08465 .BD. Ricardo N. M. J. Páscoa and David S. M. Ribeiro thank FCT (Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia) for funding through program DL 57/2016 – Norma transitória. This work received support from PT national funds (FCT FCT / MCTES , Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia and Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior) through the projects UIDB/50006/2020 and UIDP/50006/2020 . Financing from INTERREG V A Espanha Portugal Program (POCTEP) under the project FOODSENS was also greatly appreciated.

Financing from INTERREG V A Espanha Portugal Program (POCTEP) under the project FOODSENS was also greatly appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Normalized PL spectra of CDs (blue line), MPA-AIS QDs (green line) and TMA-AIS QDs (red line). PL decay curves of b) ternary QDs and c) CDs synthesized nanomaterials. The black line represents the obtained prompt.
PL spectra of the sensing platforms (λex = 400 nm) by adding increasing concentrations of histamine. a) MPA-AIS QDs; b) TMA-AIS QDs and; c) CDs.

a) Stern-Volmer plots showing the ratio of the emission intensities for MPA-AIS QDs (orange circles) and CDs (blue squares) upon the interaction with histamine. b) Individual PL emission intensity evolution throughout 15 min for ternary QDs (orange circles) and blue-emitting CDs (blue squares).

a) PL decay curves of MPA-stabilized ternary QDs (λem = 662 nm) alone and by adding increasing concentrations of histamine. b) Stern-Volmer plot exhibiting the PL intensities ratio (orange circles) and the PL lifetimes ratio (blue triangles).
PL spectra of blue emitting CDs (I), MPA-stabilized AIS QDs (II) and combined nanoprobe with different CDs factor dilutions: a) 60000 (III) and b) 30000 (IV).

Three-dimensional spectra of the PL emission as a function of wavelength and time of the ratiometric probes (a and c) in the absence (blank) and (b and d) by adding a 5.32 mg L−1 histamine standard solution. Fig. 6a and b were obtained with CDs diluted 1:30000 while Fig. 6c and d were obtained with CDs diluted 1:60000.

Three-dimensional spectra of the PL emission as a function of wavelength and time of the ratiometric probe upon interacting with a 6.84 mg L−1 histamine standard solution (a and c) and respective second-dimensional plot of the PL emission versus time (b and d) during 15 min (a and b) and 5 min of spectral acquisition (c and d).

a) Histamine calibration curve plot with concentrations ranging from 0 to 10.26 mg L−1; b) histamine predicted concentrations' plot versus the real histamine concentrations' considering the spiked samples; and c) elliptical join confidence region (at 95% confidence level) for the slope and intercept of the regression for predicted histamine which was obtained through the application of the N-PLS model using 2 LV's and a spectral acquisition of 5 min.
Maximum emission wavelength (λ max.), PL lifetime and QY values of each synthesized quantum dot.
The obtained results considering the calibration and validation set using two chemometric models.

Results of histamine predicted concentrations' considering the spiked samples and respective figures of merit through the application of the N-PLS model using 2 LV's and a spectral acquisition of 5 min.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: R2 P: determination coefficient of prediction; RMSEP: root mean square error of prediction; RE: Relative percentage error in concentration predictions; IFV – intensity fluorescence values; LOD: Limit of detection; LOQ: Limit of quantification.

head.ce:title: A ratiometric carbon dots/AgInS2 quantum dots sensing probe for the kinetic determination of histamine resorting to N-PLS chemometric model

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biogenic amines are compounds that play several crucial roles in the human body but can also cause serious health problems when present at high concentrations in foodstuffs. Therefore, it is important to develop rapid, simple, cost-effective, and reliable analytical techniques for their monitoring, acting as indicators of food quality and preventing potential risks. In this sense, carbon dots and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA)-capped AgInS2 (AIS) quantum dots were used as fluorescent probes to implement a ratiometric sensing platform, coupled to chemometrics, for the kinetic fluorometric determination of histamine in foodstuffs using second-order data. The obtained data were analysed through unfolded partial least squares (U-PLS) and N-way partial least squares (N-PLS). The model optimization revealed that the application of N-PLS yielded the best results and that a kinetic spectral acquisition for 5 min was enough to ensure reliable measurements. The developed method was evaluated using spiked samples of different complex matrices (tuna, tomato and hake fish), at different concentration levels, and the obtained results, in terms of figures of merit, confirmed its suitability, accuracy and effectiveness. In fact, a relative percentage error below 10%, with a coefficient of determination for the prediction higher than 0.9, was obtained, together with a LOD and LOQ of 1.26 and 3.80 mg L−1, respectively, which are much lower than the maximum threshold value established by European Union legislation for histamine in fishery products (200 mg kg−1). These results were obtained with samples containing uncalibrated species and without using toxic reagents, which attest the suitability of the procedure. Globally, the developed methodology proved to be accurate and much simpler and faster than the reference procedures.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Biogenic amines (BAs) are compounds that contain one or more amino groups with low molecular weights (Jaguey-Hernández et al., 2021). These compounds play a crucial role not only in multiple physiological functions, such as body temperature monitoring and stomach pH and blood pressure regulation but also in other important actions related to the metabolic, gastrointestinal and nervous systems (Omer et al., 2021). BAs are biologically active molecules synthesized by enzymatic decarboxylation of free amino acids or as a consequence of the amination and transamination of aldehydes and ketones (Jaguey-Hernández et al., 2021).

In some food products, e.g. fish, the levels of BAs can be used as quality indicators in terms of freshness and adequacy for consumption, while in other cases, like cheese, they are related to organoleptic characteristics (Nadeem et al., 2019). Nevertheless, high concentration levels of BAs in foodstuffs can cause negative effects on human health, with harmful toxicological effects (Omer et al., 2021). These include respiratory problems, headaches, heart palpitations, alterations in the normal blood tension, flushing, urticaria, abdominal pains and cramps, diarrhoea, heart failure, vomiting, brain haemorrhage, renal intoxication, and gastric problems (Omer et al., 2021).

All foodstuffs have proteins or free amino acids which could lead to the formation of BAs, whose concentration differs depending on the presence of microorganisms and the type of the food. Indeed, the quantity of these compounds is an indicator of the level of microbiological contamination in food products. The most representative BAs are histamine, putrescine, cadaverine and tyramine (Mohammed et al., 2016).

Histamine is the only BA with a maximum limit established in the EU and USA (Mohammed et al., 2016; Nadeem et al., 2019). In effect, high levels of histamine have been found in food, such as fish and other seafood products (Jaguey-Hernández et al., 2021), as a consequence of lack of freshness, deficient storage and/or hygienically inadequate manufacturing processes. Beyond the monitoring of the formation of histamine in products of animal origin, the control of this compound in histamine-rich food, like tomatoes and spinach, is of paramount importance (Bolygo et al., 2000; San Mauro Martin et al., 2016). In fact, tomatoes contain histamine due to certain processing and storage conditions, such as high temperatures, long storage times, or mechanical damage. By monitoring the level of histamine in tomatoes, spinach and eggplants, food manufacturers can guarantee that their products are harmless for consumption and meet regulatory standards for histamine levels (Bolygo et al., 2000; San Mauro Martin et al., 2016). For this reason, hundreds of analytical approaches have been developed to control histamine levels in foodstuffs. Capillary electrochromatography (CEC) and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) are the most used techniques for histamine monitoring in food (Mohammed et al., 2016; Nadeem et al., 2019). Although the chromatographic-based methodologies are highly sensitive and specific towards histamine, they require not only expensive equipment and special laboratory conditions but also skilled operators (Castro, Saraiva, et al., 2021).

Alternatively, optical methodologies involving the use of quantum dots (QDs) which, because of their fascinating optical and electrochemical proprieties, have also been explored as sensing elements in photoluminescence (PL)-based methodologies (Castro, Ribeiro, & Santos, 2021; Castro, Saraiva, et al., 2021). However, the poor QDs' selectivity has been emphasized as the major drawback in most of the reported approaches, which could impair the detection of the analyte in complex samples (Castro, Ribeiro, & Santos, 2021; Castro, Saraiva, et al., 2021). For that reason, some strategies have been suggested to overcome this problem. One of them, involves the use of separation techniques to extract the analyte from the sample matrix, thus removing the amount of potential interferents. Nevertheless, these procedures are usually laborious and time-consuming, and could require strong acids, heating, auxiliary oxidants, and organic solvents (Hyötyläinen, 2009; Švarc-Gajić & Stojanović, 2011). Another strategy to improve QDs' selectivity is through their surface functionalization with a recognition element, such as antibodies, nucleic acids, or molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs), which assures a specific binding capability towards the analyte (Castro, Saraiva, et al., 2021; Fang et al., 2022). Despite the high efficiency of QD's functionalization, this strategy increases the cost of the proposed methodology and demands complex additional procedures. Finally, the use of multi-emitter nanoprobes with multi-point detection, in which the PL information is analysed by suitable chemometric models (enabling the quantification of the analytes in complex samples) is another possible strategy (Bittar et al., 2017; Castro et al., 2020; Castro, Páscoa, et al., 2021; Castro, Saraiva, et al., 2021; Castro et al., 2023; Ribeiro et al., 2019). Moreover, with this strategy, the presence of interfering and uncalibrated species in complex samples and other issues related to light scattering and inner filter effects can be circumvented using appropriate chemometric models to analyse the PL data.

Concerning the latter strategy, one of the most explored multi-emitters nanoprobes consists of the combination of distinctly-sized QDs (emitting at distinct wavelengths) in a ratiometric sensing platform (Bigdeli et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2016). In this regard, through the combination of two fluorophores in the same assay, two sensing strategies could be established: i) a ratiometric platform encompassing as reference a non-reactive fluorophore and a reactive fluorophore whose PL intensity is modulated by the target analyte, and; ii) a nanoplatform comprising two reacting fluorophores, which, in the presence of the target analyte, undergo a PL intensity modulation in opposite directions. In both cases, the intensity ratio between the PL signals can be calculated allowing to prevent analytical shortcomings such as background fluorescence, excitation source oscillations and some matrix effects in complex samples (Bigdeli et al., 2019; Castro et al., 2019; Castro et al., 2022; Wu et al., 2016).

The selection of how data can be acquired and the most suitable chemometric model depends on the work's objective. Effectively, when assessing food, environmental or biological samples, which have complex matrices, the acquisition and analysis of second-order data, as three-dimensional data sets (e.g., PL kinetic data and excitation-emission matrix (EEM)), by adequate chemometric models, enables to obtain the second-order advantage. This means that the analyte can be accurately and selectively determined in complex matrix samples in the presence of interfering species, even if those interferents were not included in the calibration set. Having this in mind, we decided to use kinetic PL data (which are second-order data) to circumvent the possible interference of unexpected and uncalibrated species in real samples (Castro, Páscoa, et al., 2021; Castro, Saraiva, et al., 2021; Castro et al., 2023). The acquisition of EEM data involves the use of more sophisticated fluorometers when compared to PL kinetic data that can be obtained in the most common fluorometers. This method when compared to the abovementioned reference procedures commonly used for the quantification of histamine (based on CEC or HPLC) uses less expensive equipment, does not use toxic reagents and therefore does not produce toxic effluents, which can be considered more environmentally friendly.

In this work, carbon dots (CDs) and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA)-capped AgInS2 (AIS) QDs were used to implement a ratiometric sensing platform, for the monitoring of histamine in foodstuffs. To avoid the presence of uncalibrated and possible interferent species in the food samples, a kinetic PL approach was thoroughly assessed to achieve the second-order advantage. The successive PL spectra allowed to obtain additional information about the interaction process which were then processed through N-way partial least squares (N-PLS) and unfolded partial least squares (U-PLS).

The chemicals used in this work were of analytical grade without extra purification or any modification. The solutions and standards were prepared using ultrapure water from a Milli-Q system with a conductivity ≤0.1 μS cm−1.

For the preliminary assays, the histamine stock solution was obtained by dissolving 15.2 mg of the BAs standard reagent (C5H9N3, Sigma-Aldrich®, ≥97%, St. Louis, MO, USA) in 100 mL of deionized water. Then, an intermediate histamine solution was prepared for the calibration step by diluting 10 × the stock solution (15.2 mg L−1).

The ternary QDs were obtained by performing the microwave-assisted aqueous synthesis route described in the scientific literature for thiomalic acid (TMA) (Castro, Páscoa, et al., 2021, 2022; Ribeiro et al., 2020) and MPA (Castro et al., 2020; Soares et al., 2020) capping agents. Additionally, CDs were prepared following the protocol proposed by Castro et al. (Castro et al., 2019; Castro et al., 2022).

For the synthesis of the semiconductors ternary AIS QDs, silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.9999%), thiomalic acid (TMA, C4H6O4S, ≥99.0%), mercaptopropionic acid (MPA, C3H6O2S, 99%) and ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 28.0–30.0% NH3 basis) were purchased from Sigma–Aldrich® (St. Louis, MO, USA), while sodium sulfide (Na2S·9H2O, 98+%) and indium chloride (InCl3, 99.995%) were acquired from Acros organics™ (New Jersey, USA). For CDs synthesis, the citric acid (C6H8O7, ≥99.5%) and ethylenediamine (N2C2H6, ≥99.5%) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich® (St. Louis, MO, USA).

The spiked food samples were prepared by weighing a proper amount of tuna muscle, hake fish and tomato (approximately 2 g). Then, 5 mL of deionized water was used to extract the compounds of each foodstuff by using mechanical stirring for 2 h. Subsequently, the pH of the solution was adjusted nearly to 7 using a 0.1 mol L−1 ammonium hydroxide solution and the resultant solution was spiked with a proper amount of histamine. Finally, the solution was filtered using a syringe filter i.d.0.20 μm (Corning®).

A microwave irradiation synthesizer from CEM Discover SP® was used for the synthesis of AIS QDs. All the operations were controlled by a computer using Synergy™ software (Matthews, NC, US). This microwave was equipped with an active cooling system (PowerMAX™), an integrated infrared (IR) sensor and an automated pressure control/sensing system (ActiVent™). A pH-meter GLP 22 (CRISON) was used to measure the pH of the solutions.

The emission spectra (excitation wavelength fixed at 400 and emission ranging from 460 to 800 nm) were acquired through a Fluorolog®-3 spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, NJ, USA) in an air-conditioned room (19 °C). The time-resolved PL measurements were collected using a DeltaFlex™ TCSPC lifetime spectrofluorometer (Horiba Scientific, Kyoto, Japan). The absolute PL quantum yields (QY) were acquired using a Quantaurus QY C11347-11 spectrometer (Hamamatsu) equipped with an integrating sphere.

Seeking at implementing the kinetic assay, the CDs intermediate solution was obtained by diluting 100 μL of the crude solution with ultrapure water in a final volume of 150 mL (ratio of 1:1500). Additionally, the preparation of MPA-AIS intermediate solutions was daily carried out, by diluting 200 μL of the as-synthesized solution in a final volume of 2 mL (ratio of 1:10).

Then, 100 μL of CDs intermediate solution and appropriate amounts of histamine intermediate solution (15.2 mg L−1) and deionized water were added. To finish, 50 μL of MPA-AgInS2 intermediate solution was added, completing the volume to 2000 μL in a quartz cuvette (1 cm of optical path).

The obtained solution was shaken and the emission spectra were registered every minute for up to 5 min (fixing the excitation wavelength at 400 nm and varying the emission wavelength from 460 to 800 nm). The excitation and emission slit widths were set at 5.0 nm.

The obtained kinetic fluorescence data has a three-dimensional size, A x B x C where A represents the samples’ number, B represents the fluorescence emission wavelength and C represents the time, and for this reason, it can be considered as second-order data. In this sense, two different chemometric models, namely U-PLS (Wold et al., 1987) and N-PLS (Bro, 1996), were tested for analysing the obtained data. Both models were used for developing quantification models against histamine concentrations. The calibration set was composed of 13 standard samples up to 10.26 mg L−1 of histamine, while the validation set was composed of 6 spiked samples of tuna, tomato, and hake fish, at two different levels of concentration.

The best number of latent variables (LV) was assessed through the leave-one-out cross-validation method following Haaland and Thomas criterion (Haaland & Thomas, 1988) using only the calibration set (standard samples). In a few words, this criterion states that the best number of LV should be selected according to the prediction error sum of squares (PRESS), specifically, the best number of LV should be selected when PRESS values are not statistically different (F-ratio probability below 0.75) to the minimum PRESS value from models using a higher number of LV. The PRESS value expresses the suitability of a PLS model against the concentrations estimated. The developed calibration models were evaluated according to the root mean square error of calibration (RMSEC) and the determination coefficient (R2 C). Then, the validation samples (food spiked samples) were projected on the optimised models to attest their accuracy. The models were then evaluated using the root mean square error of prediction (RMSEP), relative percentage error for concentration predictions (RE), limit-of-determination (LOD), limit-of-quantification (LOQ) and coefficient of determination for the prediction (R2 P). These figures of merit were calculated according to the equations found in Allegrini et al. (Allegrini & Olivieri, 2012).
The multivariate data analysis was performed using Matlab version 8.3 (MathWorks, Natick, USA) and the MVC2 routine (Olivieri et al., 2009).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of AIS ternary QDs and carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, different QDs were prepared with the objective of obtaining dissimilar reactivities towards histamine, and with this implement a ratiometric sensing platform for the quantification of this biogenic amine in foodstuffs. Thus, QDs with distinct natures (semiconductor and carbon dots) and also semiconductor QDs whose surfaces were passivated with different capping ligands, were prepared and tested.

According to their chemical nature, it was anticipated that the CDs would demonstrate a higher suitability to act as a reference signal in the ratiometric assays, since they have previously shown chemical inertness towards similar analytes (Castro et al., 2019; Castro, Ribeiro, & Santos, 2021; Castro et al., 2022). On the other hand, it was also anticipated that the semiconductor QDs passivated with capping ligands with diverse terminal functional groups, will have a distinctive reactivity for the selected analyte (Castro et al., 2020; Ribeiro et al., 2019). For these reasons, CDs and two distinctly capped (MPA and TMA) AgInS2 (AIS) QDs were synthesized to evaluate their reactivity upon the interaction with histamine.

The optical properties of the synthesized TMA and MPA-capped Cd-free QDs and the CDs were characterized (Table 1 ) by resorting to time-resolved and steady-state fluorimetry and their quantum yield was also assessed.

Analysis of each individual PL spectra (Fig. 1 a) showed that the nanoparticles exhibited broad but symmetric emission bands. The typical broad emission band of AIS QDs can be ascribed to distinct relaxation pathways, related to the presence of mid-gap defect states within the band gap (Castro et al., 2020; Castro et al., 2022). Additionally, no significant overlap was observed for the emission spectra of the assayed nanomaterials, which enabled the combination of two QDs in a ratiometric probe maintaining the original well-defined emission bands.

The obtained data, through the time-resolved PL measurements (Fig. 1b and c) of the distinct nanoparticles, indicated that the PL decay could be appropriately fitted with three exponential kinetics. Moreover, as expected, the composition of the nanoparticles and the nature of the surface capping ligand play a preponderant role in the PL lifetime values.

The as-prepared QDs were evaluated in terms of reactivity with histamine, seeking to select the most appropriate for implementing the PL ratiometric nanoprobe regarding its quantification. Therefore, the dilution factor of all PL nanoparticles was adapted to obtain an identical initial PL intensity. Consequently, a dilution factor of 1:400,1:53.3,1:300000 was used for MPA-AIS, TMA-AIS and CDs, respectively.

In complex samples like foodstuffs, some unknown chemical species can influence the PL signal and impair the determination of histamine (Sadecka & Tothova, 2007). The use of second-order data could be explored to circumvent this problem, due to the second-order advantage. Bearing this in mind, the implementation of a PL kinetic approach was considered an interesting strategy to acquire second-order data. Since it was essential to guarantee the stability of the selected nanomaterials during the assays, their stability was evaluated for 15 min to verify if it was indeed possible of implementing the referred kinetic approach. The analysis of the results (Fig. 3b) showed a slight decrease in the emission intensity of MPA-AIS (∼26%) and of CDs (∼18%), after 15 min, which did not impair the implementation of the kinetic strategy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of PL quenching of MPA-AIS QDs upon interaction with histamine
ce:section.ce:para: The mechanism of the PL quenching of the MPA-AIS QDs upon interacting with the histamine was carefully assessed. Thus, the PL lifetimes of MPA-AIS QDs before and after the interaction with increasing histamine concentrations (up to 19 mg L−1), were evaluated to clarify if the PL quenching effect could be ascribed to a dynamic or a static process (Fig. 4 a).

As it can be seen, the PL lifetime decay values of MPA-capped AIS QDs diminished for increasing concentrations of histamine, which can be described by the following equation:
τ 0
τ
= 0.061
(
± 0.004
)
×
[
h i s t a m i n e
]
+ 0.88
(
± 0.04
)
n = 7
when comparing the slope of this equation (0.061 ± 0.004) with the obtained by the Stern-Volmer equation (0.064 ± 0.004), one can observe that both were very similar (Fig. 4b). This means that the quenching phenomenon triggered by histamine can be attributed to dynamic processes, relying on collisional interactions between the quencher and the excited fluorophore. This led to the depopulation of the QDs' excited state without PL emission, resulting in the reduction of the mean decay lifetime of the whole excited-state population. Additionally, considering the unquenched lifetime of MPA-AIS QDs (491 ns) and the dynamic quenching constant (KD = 6793 L mol−1), the bimolecular quenching rate constant (kq) was estimated at 1.38 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1. The obtained kq value suggests that histamine efficiently quenches AIS QDs, since this parameter expresses not only the quenching efficiency, but also the receptiveness of the PL nanoparticles to the quencher (Lakowicz, 2006).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of CDs/MPA-ternary-QDs ratiometric sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: For the fluorometric monitoring of histamine, two different ratiometric sensing platforms were tested. With this purpose, different ratios of AIS-QDs and CDs were mixed to evaluate the ability of the multi-emitter ratiometric sensing platform to probe histamine. In these ratiometric probes, the CDs dilution varied (1:60000 and 1:30000, respectively). The obtained PL spectra of the CDs, with distinct dilution factors, and of the ternary QDs, individually, as well as of the spectra obtained with the multi-emitter nanoprobes are depicted in Fig. 5 .

The intensity of the PL emission of MPA-AIS QDs and CDs, when combined in the dual-emitter nanoprobes, was lower than that observed for each nanomaterial when alone. This occurs because both nanomaterials shared the same excitation light source. Therefore, an inner filter effect between each other can occur with the consequent PL signal inhibition.
When a dilution factor of 1:30000 in the CDs solution was used, a similar emission intensity between the CDs and AIS peak was obtained (Fig. 5b).

The two distinct dual-emitter ratiometric sensing platforms were evaluated using the calibration standards (13 calibration standards within 0 and 10.26 mg L−1) to verify which ratiometric probe yields the best results, through the U-PLS model. An example of the blank and a standard calibration with a histamine concentration of 5.32 mg L−1 is given in Fig. 6 . From the analysis of Fig. 6 it is obvious that the CDs diluted 1:60000 (Fig. 6c and d) have a much lower fluorescence signal than the MPA-stabilized AIS QDs. However, for the ratiometric probe with the CDs diluted 1:30000 (Fig. 6a and b) the fluorescence signal of both QDs is similar.

The obtained results, in terms of R2 C and RMSEC, demonstrated that the ratiometric probe with the CDs diluted 1:30000 was the best option. In fact, a R2 C of 0.945 and a RMSEC of 0.996 mg L−1 were obtained with this probe, while for the ratiometric probe using CDs diluted 1:60000 the R2 C and RMSEC were of 0.904 and 1.333 mg L−1, respectively. In this sense, the ratiometric probe using CDs diluted 1:30000 were used for further studies.

The next step involved the evaluation of two chemometric models, namely N-PLS and U-PLS, as abovementioned, by using the validation samples and by testing different time intervals. The attained results are shown in Table 2 .

Three conclusions can be drawn from the obtained results: the N-PLS model presented better results in terms of prediction than the U-PLS model, 5 min of kinetic spectral acquisition were enough to obtain accurate results and both chemometric models demonstrated to be able to accurately determine histamine in complex matrices samples. During the analysis of the three-dimensional spectra of standards and validation samples, it was evident that the quenching effect upon the interaction of the ratiometric probe and histamine was more pronounced during the first minutes. This is evident in more detail in Fig. 7 .

The second-dimensional plot of the PL emission spectra shows (Fig. 7b) that the biggest differences in the intensity fluorescence values occur in the first minutes. After 5 min, the changes in the intensity fluorescence values are less pronounced.
Therefore, the chemometric model that yielded the best results was N-PLS when using a spectral acquisition of 5 min.

The developed models’ accuracy was evaluated using spiked samples (validation samples) of tuna, tomato, and hake fish, at two different levels of concentration. These samples can contain interferents which can influence histamine quantification. However, as the acquired data are second-order data, the effect of uncalibrated interferents may be minimised through the use of residual bilinearization (RBL) (Olivieri, 2005). The obtained results without the application of RBL demonstrated that the existence of unforeseen species in the spiked samples did not hinder the quantification of histamine. Moreover, the validation samples residuals were comparable to the values of the instrumental noise. In this sense, it was not necessary to achieve the second-order advantage through the application of the RBL algorithm.
The calibration curve, the validation samples results and the obtained figures of merit, considering the best N-PLS model and using 5 min of spectral acquisition, are shown in Fig. 8 and Table 3 .

The interface used (MVC2) for the N-PLS model retrieves the figures of merit and, for each sample, a LOD and LOQ are obtained. In this sense, the LOD and LOQ values exhibited in Table 3 correspond to the average of the respective maximum values. As can be seen through the analysis of the figures of merit, the presented methodology revealed to be accurate for histamine quantification in different complex matrices. In fact, the RE was below 10% and the R2 P was higher than 0.9, confirming the suitability of the developed methodology. Moreover, the LOD and LOQ of 1.26 and 3.80 mg L−1, respectively, obtained in the developed methodology are lower than the maximum threshold value established by the European Union (EU) for histamine in fishery products, which is 200 mg kg−1 (DeBeeR et al., 2021).

Therefore, the herein described methodology offers a simple approach based on a ratiometric fluorescent probe coupled to chemometric models for histamine quantification in complex matrices. The obtained results evidenced the accuracy and suitability of the proposed methodology.

Compared with the existing standard methods based on HPLC or CEC (being HPLC the most used for the determination of histamine), this method is quicker than almost the other methods, simpler, less laborious, does not involve any reactants/equipment preparation for analysis (e.g. in HPLC there is a need to remove the oxygen from the mobile phase, in CEC we need to prepare the capillaries), does not use toxic reagents and therefore does not produce toxic effluents, being able of quantifying histamine in the presence of uncalibrated species (due to second-order advantage) (Mohammed et al., 2016; Nadeem et al., 2019). However, this method involves the preparation of samples for pH adjustment, does not quantify different biogenic amines at once and has a LOD and LOQ higher than most of the HPLC and CEC method. Nonetheless, the LOD and LOQ obtained in this work, as abovementioned, are much lower than the maximum threshold value established by the EU.

A kinetic approach exploring the interaction process between histamine and a fluorescent ratiometric sensing platform based on the mixture of CDs and MPA-capped AIS QDs was developed and demonstrated to be efficient for the quantification of the histamine in different complex matrices by resorting to the chemometric analysis of the obtained PL data. As these data are second-order data, the quantification of histamine in the presence of uncalibrated species could be attainable through the second-order advantage. Two chemometric models, namely U-PLS and N-PLS, were tested. The obtained results allowed to conclude that both chemometric models retrieved an accurate quantification of histamine, although the N-PLS model presented better results in terms of prediction than the U-PLS model. Additionally, it was also possible to conclude that the first 5 min of the kinetic spectral acquisition were enough for the accurate quantification of histamine. Indeed, a RE below 10% with a R2 P higher than 0.9 was obtained, together with a LOD and LOQ of 1.26 and 3.80 mg L−1, respectively, which is much below the maximum threshold value established by the European Union for histamine in fishery products (200 mg kg−1). The developed methodology proved to be accurate, reliable, much simpler, less laborious and more environmentally friendly than the reference procedures.

This work received financial support from PT national funds (FCT/MCTES, Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia and Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior) through the projects UIDB/50006/2020 and UIDP/50006/2020 and INTERREG V A Espanha Portugal Program (POCTEP) under the project FOODSENS.

Rafael.C. Castro: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Data curation, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Visualization. Ricardo N.M.J. Páscoa: Visualization, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft. M.Lúcia M.F.S. Saraiva: Validation, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Sara.C. Cunha: Funding acquisition, Data curation. João L.M. Santos: Funding acquisition, Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. David S.M. Ribeiro: Visualization, Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, All authors have read and agreed to the published version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Rafael C. Castro thanks FCT/MCTES (Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia and Ministério da Ciência, Tecnologia e Ensino Superior) and ESF (European Social Fund) through NORTE 2020 (Programa Operacional Região Norte) for his PhD grant ref. 2020.08465.BD. Ricardo N. M. J. Páscoa and David S. M. Ribeiro thank FCT (Fundação para a Ciência e Tecnologia) for funding through program DL 57/2016 – Norma transitória. Financing from INTERREG V A Espanha Portugal Program (POCTEP) under the project FOODSENS was also greatly appreciated.
